preamble: The phonetic transformation from the voiceless postalveolar affricate /tʃ/ to the voiceless alveolar affricate /ts/ involves a fronting of the place of articulation. This shift moves the point of constriction from the postalveolar region, behind the alveolar ridge, to the alveolar ridge itself. While both sounds are voiceless affricates, the change results in a sharper, more fronted articulation.
phoneticEffects: Fronting; change in place of articulation from postalveolar to alveolar
sources: Grierson, G. A. (1919). Linguistic Survey of India, Vol. VIII, Part II: Specimens of the Dardic or Pisacha Language. Calcutta: Superintendent Government Printing.
